Now that you know how to shrug off the first punch, you may need to learn more about how to deal with the continued barrage of punches. Count on the fact that some people just won't get the message the first time. They may be caught up in a toxic cycle, or just be determined to be counterproductive for whatever secondary gain or hidden agenda they have. Be aware. There's a strategy called strategic umbrage. Think of this as the tactical expression of anger. It's important to know how the door swings both ways. First, will it be used on you? Yes. That may be what you're experiencing now. It's been shown to be effective at getting short-term concessions. Because of that, it's very seductive to some negotiators. Second, should you use it? Should you fight fire with a flamethrower? Let's break this down. Umbridge or anger? Strategic or not? It's a poor strategy for two reasons. Number one, concessions gained through this always have implementation problems. Always. So what this is is a shortcut to trouble. Number two, anger is a toxin that degrades relationships and triggers poor decision making. There's an old saying, give a speech when you're angry and you'll give the best speech you'll ever regret. So when you find yourself in a negotiation that's far from a resolution, is going nowhere fast and you are continually under attack, you probably need to set some clear boundaries to get your counterpart out of this approach. Assertive moves are completely acceptable. They're just enhanced by tactical empathy. And more than anything, the initial enhancement comes through your tone of voice. You don't have to be a jerk to assert. And most notably, you don't have to sound like a jerk to assert. So if they won't budge from their ridiculous position or approach, rather than getting anxious or angry or even storming out, take a deep breath and reply with the late night FM DJ voice with, I'm sorry, that just doesn't work for me. Tonality is crucial here. This is the late night FM DJ voice delivered in slow measure tones. It's critical you avoid sounding condescending here. Unfazed, that's the way you want to sound. Unrattled, calm is contagious. Be aware, you'll be tempted to use this voice to punish them a little, to talk down to them. Tempting, it won't help. If there are some words that are getting ready to come out of your mouth and you can just taste how good it's going to feel for you to say them, that's an indicator they're probably the wrong words. With a good late night FM DJ voice, you trigger your counterparts mirror neurons into working for you. This voice slows their brain. Be prepared to hit them with this voice at least three times. You'll watch the effects slowly wash over them a little more each time. The words we've chosen here is a version of what's called an eye message. Eye, the first person singular pronoun, is a great tool to set a boundary without escalating into confrontation. If you feel an offer isn't fair or that you're being taken advantage of, you can employ this to help give you a breathing room without being harsh. When you say, I'm sorry, that just doesn't work. The word eye strategically focuses your counterparts attention onto you long enough to make a point. The traditional usage of the tool is to use eye to hit the pause button and step out of a bad dynamic. A short dose of tactical empathy to proceed this might be, that's very generous of you. I'm sorry, that just doesn't work for me. Remember empathy is defined as the other person's perspective. Often times, completely understanding something is also knowing what it isn't. This definition isn't reality, accuracy, or the agreement of the truth. It's the other side's perspective. It's their truth. In their bizarre world, in this type of situation, I guarantee they feel you're lucky they're talking to you at all. That, by their definition, makes them generous. It sounds crazy to call someone generous that seems to be intentionally difficult or combative. That doesn't change the fact that from where they sit, they feel they're being kind. When you're outlining what other people see the world as, it may be helpful to know that it isn't necessarily supposed to make sense to you. The trained negotiator can verbalize it anyway. The other tactical beauty here is that labeling positives always enhances those positives. Claiming them is generous and you will enhance them into generosity. Another great response from when your counterpart is engaging in unproductive behavior, such as yelling or name calling, is to say, I can't hear you when you yell, or I can't hear you when you call his names, or I can't hear you when you and then fill in the blank citing the unproductive behavior your counterpart is hitting you with. Choose a late night FM DJ voice and repeat as necessary. This spin causes their actions to be felt by them. Appropriate and fair confrontation. Appropriate and fair means it's called for, and you're simultaneously avoiding embarrassing the other side. Yes, it can be done. And why do you want to avoid embarrassing them? No matter how much they deserve it? What leaves a stain that pretty much never goes away. Long term, it just doesn't serve you. Here's how you do this. The first definition of confrontation is a focused comparison. So how do you do that in a negotiation? Label a counterproductive action with the stated goal that action contradicts. Hold them up for comparison with a, how do those line up? Use a genuinely curious late night FM DJ voice. For example, you stated you want to come to an agreement and you're against our suggestion. How do those things line up? How do those things fit together? As we move on, allow me to highlight to you again the importance of maintaining a collaborative relationship. Even when you're setting boundaries, your response must be expressed with a firm yet empathetic demeanor. Not anger, not punishment, not condescension. Think of this process as an S curve. You've hit a dead end and you can't go forward from here. The first portion of an S curve feels like a U turn. So what this does is allow you to back up a little so you can find a way around. This would be more productive than hitting your head against a brick wall, trying to see which will break first. The adversary is the situation, not the person. This very simple and basic mindset will help you to avoid escalating emotions and keep your approach a collaborative one, encouraging them to articulate their objections clearly, without attacking you as your best chance of success. Then together, you can overcome the obstacles and find the best deal possible. If they're vested in the deal, they are far more likely to implement.
